refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report energy metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the enablement of measurement and visibility—the system's responsibility. Achievement of the target is a validation criterion (acceptance), not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the data and mechanisms; meeting the target depends on design choices, operational practices, and external factors. The FR is now in the problem domain (what must be measured and reported) rather than prescribing how or what technology to use.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can continuously or periodically measure energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent task units), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric through operator interfaces and reporting systems. Verify that reported values are traceable to calibrated sensors and that the measurement window aligns with the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: Measure total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
Measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task-specific units) during the same operational period.
Calculate and derive energy efficiency metric (energy per unit output) from measured values.
Report energy efficiency metric to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer) in real-time or near-real-time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance report.

DP2: Deploy real-time energy metering on the power supply circuit and work-output sensors on the load/displacement mechanism; stream both signals to a local data aggregator that computes rolling energy-per-ton metrics and flags exceedances; persist results for post-evaluation audit.

DP3: Establish a measurement protocol that decouples energy acquisition (via power monitoring) from work quantification (via load/distance sensors); define a common time-synchronization reference; compute energy_per_ton offline after data collection period closes; validate against target with statistical confidence bounds.

DP4: Instrument energy consumption at the system boundary (input power); instrument work output at the functional boundary (load moved × distance); implement a measurement-to-reporting pipeline that normalizes both signals, handles sensor drift and calibration, and produces energy_per_ton with uncertainty quantification; compare point estimate and confidence interval against E_target.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Instrument energy consumption at the system boundary (input power); instrument work output at the functional boundary (load moved × distance); implement a measurement-to-reporting pipeline that normalizes both signals, handles sensor drift and calibration, and produces energy_per_ton with uncertainty quantification; compare point estimate and confidence interval against E_target.

potential_sources: Energy measurement and work-output measurement are logically independent (different physical domains: electrical vs. mechanical); DP4 decouples them at the measurement layer and couples only at the analytics/reporting layer, which is appropriate.
Sensor calibration and drift correction are localized to the measurement-to-reporting pipeline; this avoids tight coupling between sensor hardware and business logic.
Time synchronization is implicit in the pipeline design but not over-specified; allows flexibility in implementation (e.g., synchronized clocks, event-driven correlation, or post-hoc alignment).
Uncertainty quantification is part of the DP, reducing risk of false acceptance/rejection due to measurement noise; this is a strength, not a coupling risk.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 exhibits low coupling because: (1) energy and work measurements are acquired independently; (2) normalization and calibration are centralized in the pipeline, not scattered; (3) the comparison logic (point estimate + confidence interval vs. E_target) is decoupled from measurement acquisition. DP1 and DP2 are more tightly coupled (DP1 lacks real-time capability; DP2 conflates real-time monitoring with post-evaluation audit, creating dual-purpose complexity). DP3 is acceptable but less robust to sensor drift over the evaluation period.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy input is measurable at a single, well-defined system boundary (e.g., main power feed). RISK: If energy is supplied from multiple sources (grid + battery + generator), measurement complexity increases; DP4 must be extended to aggregate multiple energy streams.

ASSUMPTION: Work output is quantifiable as load × distance (or equivalent scalar metric). RISK: If work includes multi-dimensional outputs (e.g., load, height, speed, direction), the definition of 'ton_moved' must be clarified; DP4 assumes a single, agreed work metric.

ASSUMPTION: Sensors (power meter, load cell, displacement sensor) are calibrated before the evaluation period and remain stable. RISK: Drift over time or environmental factors (temperature, vibration, humidity) can invalidate results; DP4 mitigates this via calibration handling, but requires periodic re-calibration protocol.

ASSUMPTION: Time synchronization between energy and work signals is achievable (e.g., common clock, event timestamps). RISK: If synchronization is poor, energy-to-work correlation becomes unreliable; DP4 assumes synchronization is feasible.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined E_target is a fixed scalar value. RISK: If E_target varies by operating condition (e.g., load weight, terrain, ambient temperature), DP4 must be extended to support conditional thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to average out transient effects and sensor noise. RISK: Short evaluation windows may yield high variance in energy_per_ton; DP4 addresses this via uncertainty quantification, but assumes sufficient data volume.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not restrict sensor placement or data logging. RISK: Safety or privacy constraints may limit measurement access; DP4 assumes measurement points are accessible.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 to explicitly address: (1) Multi-source energy aggregation (if applicable); (2) Definition and validation of the work-output metric (ton_moved) in the context of actual operational scenarios; (3) Calibration and drift-correction protocol (frequency, method, acceptance criteria); (4) Time-synchronization mechanism (e.g., GPS, PTP, event-driven); (5) Confidence-interval calculation method (e.g., bootstrap, propagation of uncertainty) and decision rule for comparing against E_target (e.g., point estimate must be ≤ E_target, or 95th percentile must be ≤ E_target); (6) Data retention and audit trail requirements to support customer validation. Additionally, map DP4 to candidate IMs (e.g., dedicated energy-metering module, work-output sensor module, data-aggregation service, reporting dashboard) to ensure architectural clarity and ownership boundaries.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level energy measurement, normalization, and reporting pipeline that instruments input power at the system boundary and work output at the functional boundary, with sensor drift compensation, calibration management, and uncertainty quantification for energy efficiency metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits energy_per_ton point estimate, confidence interval, and uncertainty quantification to external monitoring and analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives task parameters (load mass, distance targets) required to compute work output and normalize energy metrics.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals when energy consumption exceeds E_target threshold or sensor calibration drift exceeds acceptable bounds, triggering safety or operational alerts.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level integration boundary responsible for end-to-end energy efficiency measurement and reporting. Input power instrumentation at system boundary and work output at functional boundary are both system-level concerns. Measurement pipeline, normalization, drift compensation, and uncertainty quantification are core to the solution's performance validation. Minimal diffusion: only exports normalized metrics via Telemetry and receives task context via Tasking.

Weak containment: Vehicle is a physical platform component, not a measurement/analytics layer. Work output (load × distance) computation requires task context and load mass from outside Vehicle. Energy normalization and uncertainty quantification are system-level concerns that would diffuse across Vehicle and external analytics. Sensor drift compensation and calibration logic would be orphaned from the measurement pipeline.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not an energy measurement system. The DP requires continuous instrumentation and normalization independent of teleoperation mode. Placing measurement pipeline in Teleoperation would fragment energy metrics across manual and autonomous modes, creating inconsistent reporting and duplicated calibration logic.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a reactive alert/monitoring layer, not a primary measurement system. While Safety Services may consume energy threshold alerts, the full measurement-to-reporting pipeline (sensor fusion, drift compensation, normalization) is not a safety service responsibility. Placing the DP here would couple energy efficiency metrics to safety logic, creating maintenance and validation complexity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of the energy measurement and reporting pipeline because it is the system-level integration boundary responsible for validating that the overall solution meets energy efficiency targets. Input power instrumentation at the system boundary and work output computation at the functional boundary are both system-level observables. The measurement-to-reporting pipeline, including sensor drift compensation, calibration, normalization, and uncertainty quantification, are core to the solution's performance validation and must be owned at the system level to ensure consistency, traceability, and independence from subsystem-specific implementations.
containment_risks: Risk: If input power instrumentation is physically located on the Vehicle (e.g., power sensor on battery), the Vehicle module may claim ownership of raw sensor data, creating ambiguity about where calibration and drift compensation logic resides. Mitigation: Establish clear interface contract: Vehicle owns raw sensor hardware; NGHE Solution owns measurement pipeline logic and normalization.

Risk: Telemetry interface may be bidirectional if external systems request re-calibration or parameter updates. Mitigation: Define Telemetry as primarily 'out' for metrics; use Tasking or a separate configuration interface for inbound calibration commands.

Risk: Safety Services may need real-time energy alerts to prevent over-consumption during critical operations. If Safety Services becomes a consumer of energy metrics, it may pressure NGHE Solution to embed safety-specific logic in the measurement pipeline. Mitigation: NGHE Solution produces energy metrics; Safety Services consumes them and applies safety thresholds independently.

Risk: Uncertainty quantification and confidence intervals require statistical models that may need tuning based on operational data. If this tuning is delegated to Vehicle or Teleoperation, the measurement pipeline becomes fragmented. Mitigation: Centralize all calibration and uncertainty model updates in NGHE Solution; expose tuning parameters via Tasking or a dedicated configuration interface.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete measurement-to-reporting pipeline that instruments both energy input (system boundary) and work output (functional boundary), normalizes signals, compensates for sensor drift, and produces energy_per_ton with uncertainty quantification for comparison against E_target. The DP is sufficiently detailed to be implementable and testable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target).

diffusion_risks: Risk: Raw sensor hardware ownership ambiguity. Vehicle module may physically host power sensors (e.g., battery current/voltage), creating potential claim of ownership over calibration and drift compensation logic. Mitigation: Establish explicit interface contract—Vehicle owns raw sensor hardware and data transmission; NGHE Solution owns all measurement pipeline logic (calibration, drift compensation, normalization).

Risk: Uncertainty quantification model tuning fragmentation. Statistical models for confidence intervals may require operational data-driven tuning. If delegated to Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems, the measurement pipeline becomes scattered across modules. Mitigation: Centralize all calibration and uncertainty model updates in NGHE Solution; expose tuning parameters via Tasking interface only.

Risk: Safety Services coupling. Safety Services may consume energy threshold alerts and pressure NGHE Solution to embed safety-specific logic (e.g., emergency shutdown thresholds) into the measurement pipeline. Mitigation: NGHE Solution produces normalized energy metrics and uncertainty bounds; Safety Services independently applies safety thresholds and triggers SafetyAlert. Keep measurement logic decoupled from safety decision logic.

Risk: Telemetry bidirectionality. If external monitoring systems request re-calibration or parameter updates via Telemetry, the interface becomes bidirectional, potentially creating control loops that fragment ownership. Mitigation: Define Telemetry as primarily 'out' (metrics only); use Tasking or a dedicated configuration interface for inbound calibration commands.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score and is the clear owner. The measurement-to-reporting pipeline is a system-level integration concern that must remain cohesive: input power instrumentation at system boundary, work output computation at functional boundary, sensor fusion, drift compensation, normalization, and uncertainty quantification are all interdependent and must be owned by a single module to ensure consistency, traceability, and independence from subsystem-specific implementations. Vehicle (0.45 containment) is a physical platform, not a measurement system; Teleoperation (0.3 containment) is a control interface; Safety Services (0.35 containment) is a reactive alert layer. None are appropriate owners. Minimal diffusion: NGHE Solution exports metrics via Telemetry and receives task context via Tasking—both are clean, unidirectional interfaces.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. NGHE Solution module boundary is well-defined and measurable. Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly verifiable via Telemetry output. Uncertainty quantification enables statistical validation over the agreed evaluation period. Sensor drift compensation and calibration logic are testable via controlled load/distance scenarios. Interface contracts (raw sensor data from Vehicle, task parameters from Tasking, alerts to SafetyAlert) are explicit and traceable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution owns the complete measurement-to-reporting pipeline. Vehicle owns raw sensor hardware and data transmission. Tasking provides load mass and distance targets. SafetyAlert consumes energy threshold signals. Telemetry exports normalized metrics. Each module has a single, well-defined responsibility. No orphaned logic or ambiguous ownership boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Formalize interface contract between Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Specify: (a) Vehicle transmits raw power sensor readings (voltage, current, timestamp) at defined sampling rate; (b) NGHE Solution owns all calibration, drift compensation, and normalization logic; (c) Vehicle does not perform any energy calculations or filtering.

Revision 2: Define Tasking interface schema explicitly. Specify: (a) Tasking transmits load mass (kg), distance target (m), and task start/end timestamps; (b) NGHE Solution uses these to compute work output (load × distance) and normalize energy metrics; (c) Tasking does not request or modify calibration parameters.

Revision 3: Establish configuration interface for calibration updates. Create a separate 'Calibration' interface (or extend Tasking) to allow inbound calibration commands (e.g., sensor offset updates, drift model parameters) only from authorized sources (e.g., maintenance systems). Telemetry remains 'out' only (metrics and uncertainty bounds).

Revision 4: Clarify SafetyAlert semantics. Specify: (a) NGHE Solution signals when energy_per_ton exceeds E_target or sensor calibration drift exceeds acceptable bounds; (b) Safety Services independently evaluates these signals and decides on operational response (e.g., alert, throttle, shutdown); (c) NGHE Solution does not embed safety decision logic.

Revision 5: Document uncertainty quantification model. Specify: (a) confidence interval method (e.g., bootstrap, Bayesian); (b) sensor error sources (accuracy, drift, quantization); (c) calibration procedure and frequency; (d) acceptance criteria for model validation (e.g., coverage probability ≥ 95%).

Revision 6: Add traceability requirement. NGHE Solution must log all energy measurements, calibration events, and uncertainty estimates with timestamps and metadata (task ID, load mass, distance) to enable post-hoc validation against E_target and support root cause analysis if acceptance criterion is not met.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the NGHE Solution module achieves 0.92 containment with clear ownership boundaries. However, the gate results surface six critical interface and ownership clarifications that must be formalized at the current level before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The safety criticality factor (high) amplifies the risk of proceeding without explicit interface contracts, especially around sensor ownership ambiguity, calibration parameter control, and SafetyAlert decoupling. Revising the current DP and IM specifications to incorporate these interface contracts will eliminate diffusion risks and create a stable foundation for any future sub-level decomposition. Decomposing further without these revisions risks creating orphaned logic or scattered ownership across Vehicle, Tasking, and Safety Services subsystems.

revise.why: DP4 is logically sound but lacks explicit interface specifications. Revise DP4 to include: (1) Input interface contract: Vehicle transmits raw power sensor readings (voltage, current, timestamp) at defined sampling rate; NGHE Solution owns all calibration, drift compensation, and normalization. (2) Task context interface: Tasking provides load mass, distance target, and task timestamps; NGHE Solution computes work output and normalizes energy metrics. (3) Configuration interface: Separate 'Calibration' interface for inbound calibration commands (sensor offset, drift model parameters) from authorized sources only; Telemetry remains unidirectional 'out' (metrics and uncertainty bounds). (4) Safety interface: NGHE Solution signals energy_per_ton threshold exceedance and calibration drift bounds to SafetyAlert; Safety Services independently applies safety decision logic. (5) Uncertainty quantification specification: Confidence interval method, sensor error sources, calibration procedure, and acceptance criteria (e.g., coverage probability ≥ 95%). (6) Traceability requirement: Log all measurements, calibration events, and uncertainty estimates with task metadata for post-hoc validation. These revisions transform DP4 from a functional pipeline into an architecturally explicit, interface-driven design that eliminates ambiguity and diffusion risk.

assumptions: Vehicle module physically hosts power sensors (battery current/voltage) and transmits raw readings; NGHE Solution owns all signal processing and energy calculation logic.
Tasking interface is unidirectional 'in' (load mass, distance, timestamps); NGHE Solution does not request task modifications.
Calibration updates (sensor offsets, drift model parameters) are infrequent and controlled via a dedicated configuration interface, not Telemetry.

Safety Services consumes energy threshold signals from NGHE Solution but independently applies safety decision logic (e.g., throttle, shutdown); NGHE Solution does not embed safety-specific thresholds.
Uncertainty quantification model is tuned offline using operational data and deployed as fixed parameters; runtime tuning is not required.
Telemetry interface is primarily 'out' (normalized energy metrics, uncertainty bounds, calibration status); inbound requests are rejected or routed to Calibration interface.
High safety criticality requires explicit interface contracts and clear ownership boundaries to prevent unintended coupling and ensure independent verification.
open_risks: Risk: Sensor hardware ownership ambiguity persists if Vehicle module claims ownership over calibration logic. Mitigation: Formalize interface contract explicitly stating Vehicle owns hardware, NGHE Solution owns logic.

Risk: Calibration parameter tuning fragmentation if multiple subsystems request updates. Mitigation: Centralize all calibration updates in NGHE Solution; expose tuning parameters via dedicated Calibration interface only.

Risk: Safety Services may pressure NGHE Solution to embed safety-specific thresholds (e.g., emergency shutdown). Mitigation: Clarify SafetyAlert semantics—NGHE Solution produces metrics and bounds; Safety Services applies thresholds independently.
Risk: Telemetry bidirectionality may create unintended control loops. Mitigation: Define Telemetry as 'out' only; use Calibration interface for inbound commands.
Risk: Uncertainty quantification model validation may be deferred or skipped. Mitigation: Specify acceptance criteria (e.g., coverage probability ≥ 95%) and require validation before deployment.
Risk: Traceability logging may be incomplete or inconsistent. Mitigation: Mandate logging of all measurements, calibration events, and uncertainty estimates with task metadata.
evidence_needed: Formalized interface contract between Vehicle and NGHE Solution specifying raw sensor data format, sampling rate, and transmission protocol.
Tasking interface schema defining load mass, distance target, task timestamps, and constraints on parameter modification.
Calibration interface specification (or extension to Tasking) for inbound calibration commands, authorized sources, and parameter update procedures.
SafetyAlert interface specification clarifying that NGHE Solution signals threshold exceedance and calibration drift bounds; Safety Services independently applies safety logic.
Uncertainty quantification model documentation specifying confidence interval method, sensor error sources, calibration procedure, acceptance criteria, and validation results.
Traceability and logging specification defining data schema, retention policy, and post-hoc validation procedures.

Test plan for interface contracts: (a) Vehicle-NGHE data transmission under nominal and degraded conditions; (b) Tasking parameter injection and work output computation; (c) Calibration command processing and parameter updates; (d) SafetyAlert signal generation and Safety Services response; (e) Uncertainty quantification model coverage validation over agreed evaluation period.
